items.notes: Informal but standard word for mother. More formal: 'äiti' is used in all contexts. Spoken Finnish: 'äiskä' or 'mutsi' (slang).
examples: Äiti tekee ruokaa. — Mother is making food.

items.notes: Standard word for father. Spoken Finnish: 'iskä' or 'faija' (slang).
examples: Isä on töissä. — Father is at work.

items.notes: Plural of 'vanhempi' (parent), which literally means 'older one'. Comparative form of 'vanha' (old).
examples: Minun vanhempani asuvat Helsingissä. — My parents live in Helsinki.

items.notes: Plural is 'lapset'. The stem changes in declension: lapsi → lapsen (genitive), lasta (partitive).
examples: Lapsi leikkii puistossa. — The child is playing in the park.

items.notes: Means both 'son' and 'boy'. Context determines meaning. 'Poikani' = my son.
examples: Meidän poika on koulussa. — Our son is at school.

items.notes: Primarily means 'girl'. Can be used informally for 'daughter', but 'tytär' is the specific word for daughter.
examples: Tyttö juoksee nopeasti. — The girl runs fast.

items.notes: The specific word for daughter. Stem changes: tytär → tyttären (genitive). More formal than using 'tyttö'.
examples: Heidän tyttärensä opiskelee yliopistossa. — Their daughter studies at university.

items.notes: Stem changes: veli → veljen (genitive). Plural: veljet. Informal: 'veli' is used in all registers.
examples: Minun veljeni on vanhempi kuin minä. — My brother is older than me.

items.notes: Common word for sister. Also: 'sisar' (more literary/formal). Plural: siskot.
examples: Siskoni asuu Turussa. — My sister lives in Turku.

items.notes: Core family unit. 'Perheeni' = my family. 'Suurperhe' = large family.
examples: Meillä on iso perhe. — We have a big family.

items.notes: Standard word for wife. 'Vaimoni' = my wife. In modern Finnish, 'puoliso' (spouse) is also common.
examples: Vaimoni on lääkäri. — My wife is a doctor.

items.notes: Means both 'husband' and 'man'. Context determines meaning. 'Aviomies' specifically means husband (married man).
examples: Mieheni on opettaja. — My husband is a teacher.

items.notes: Stem changes: nainen → naisen (genitive), naista (partitive). Plural: naiset.
examples: Nainen istuu penkillä. — The woman is sitting on a bench.

items.notes: Literally 'big mother' (iso + äiti). Affectionate: 'mummo' or 'mummi'. Regional variations exist.
examples: Isoäiti leipoo pullaa. — Grandmother is baking pulla (sweet bread).

items.notes: Literally 'big father' (iso + isä). Affectionate: 'pappa' or 'vaari'.
examples: Isoisä kertoo tarinoita. — Grandfather tells stories.

items.notes: Used for any aunt regardless of side of family. Also used politely by children for adult women they know.
examples: Täti toi meille lahjoja. — Aunt brought us gifts.

items.notes: Specifically father's brother. Finnish distinguishes paternal and maternal uncles. Also used by children for unfamiliar adult men.
examples: Setä asuu maalla. — Uncle (father's brother) lives in the countryside.

items.notes: Specifically mother's brother. This distinction between 'setä' and 'eno' is unique to Finnish and reflects old family structures.
examples: Eno vie meidät kalaan. — Uncle (mother's brother) takes us fishing.

items.notes: Gender-neutral word for cousin. Finnish does not distinguish between male and female cousins.
examples: Serkkuni tulee käymään kesällä. — My cousin is coming to visit in the summer.

items.notes: Used for infants. 'Vauva nukkuu' = the baby is sleeping. Also: 'taapero' = toddler.
examples: Vauva nukkuu rauhallisesti. — The baby sleeps peacefully.

items.notes: Derived from 'aika' (time). Can also be used as an adjective meaning 'adult' or 'grown-up'.
examples: Aikuinen auttaa lasta. — The adult helps the child.

items.notes: Literally 'child's child' (lapsen + lapsi). Plural: lapsenlapset. Gender-neutral.
examples: Isoäidillä on viisi lastenlasta. — Grandmother has five grandchildren.

items.notes: A close friend. In Finnish culture, calling someone 'ystävä' implies a deep bond. 'Ystävänpäivä' = Valentine's Day (Friend's Day).
examples: Hän on minun paras ystäväni. — He/she is my best friend.

items.notes: More casual than 'ystävä'. Used for acquaintances and casual friends. Very common in everyday speech.
examples: Menin kaverin kanssa elokuviin. — I went to the movies with a friend.

items.notes: Finnish neighbors tend to be reserved but helpful. 'Naapurusto' = neighborhood.
examples: Naapuri tervehtii aina ystävällisesti. — The neighbor always greets kindly.

items.notes: Plural: ihmiset (people). Stem changes: ihminen → ihmisen (genitive). A very common -nen noun.
examples: Jokainen ihminen on erilainen. — Every person is different.

items.notes: Compound: 'avo' (open) + 'puoliso' (spouse). Very common in Finland where cohabitation without marriage is widespread. 'Avoliitto' = cohabitation.
examples: Avopuolisoni on kotoisin Tampereelta. — My partner is from Tampere.

items.notes: Gender-neutral word for spouse. Literally means 'half'. Used in official contexts and modern Finnish as a gender-neutral alternative to vaimo/mies.
examples: Puolisoni ja minä matkustamme paljon. — My spouse and I travel a lot.
